{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01KJUkBKrrjeZqpaHvx8ziaR","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# The risk of clopidogrel resistance is associated with ABCB1 polymorphisms but not promoter methylation in a Chinese Han population\n     2→\n     3→## Metadata\n     4→**Authors:** Jia Su, Qinglin Yu, Hao Zhu, Xiaojing Li, Hanbin Cui, Weiping Du, Lindan Ji, Maoqing Tong, Yibo Zheng, Hongyu Xu, Jianjiang Zhang, Yunyun Zhu, Yezi Xia, Ting Liu, Qi Yao, Jun Yang, Xiaomin Chen, Jingbo Yu\n     5→**Journal:** PLoS ONE\n     6→**Date:** 2017 Mar 30\n     7→**DOI:** [10.1371/journal.pone.0174511](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0174511)\n     8→**PMID:** 28358842\n     9→**PMCID:** PMC5373545\n    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5373545/\n    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5373545/pdf/pone.0174511.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5373545/pdf/pone.0174511.pdf)\n    12→\n    13→## Abstract\n    14→\n    15→The goal of our study was to investigate the contribution of ABCB1 expression to the risk of clopidogrel resistance (CR). Platelets functions were measured using the Verify-Now P2Y12 assay. Applying Polymerase Chain Reaction–Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (PCR-RFLP), the single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) was tested. Using bisulphite pyrosequencing assay, we investigated the association of the ABCB1 DNA methylation levels and CR. It was shown that female, hypertension, and lower albumin levels increased the risk of CR (P<0.05). If patients did not have hypoproteinaemia or had hypertension, the SNP in rs1045642 was associated with CR (CC vs. TT: albumin ≥35, P = 0.042; hypertension, P = 0.045; C vs. T: albumin ≥35, P = 0.033; hypertension, P = 0.040). Additionally, the platelet inhibition of the CT+TT genotype in rs1128503 was larger than that of the CC genotype (P = 0.021). Multivariate logistic regression analysis showed that male, higher albumin and hsCRP decreased the risk of CR, and the stent size maybe positively correlated with CR. The SNP in rs1045642 was related to all-cause mortality (P = 0.024). We did not find any relationship between the methylation levels of the ABCB1 promoter and CR. In conclusions, our study indicated that ABCB1 polymorphisms might be useful in further evaluating the pathogenesis of CR.\n    16→\n    17→## Introduction\n    18→\n    19→At sites of vascular injury due to atherosclerotic plaque rupture or erosion, platelets mediate not only haemostasis but also pathologic thrombosis[[1](#pone.0174511.ref001)]. Thrombus generation (due to platelet activation and aggregation) is the main process involved in atherosclerotic vascular disease, particularly coronary artery disease (CAD)[[2](#pone.0174511.ref002)]. Therefore, antiplatelet therapy has been the cornerstone therapy in patients with coronary artery disease, especially in those undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)[[3](#pone.0174511.ref003)].\n    20→\n    21→Through inhibiting the purinergic ADP receptor P2Y12, clopidogrel reduces adenosine diphosphate-induced platelet aggregation and decreases the risk of cardiovascular events in CAD patients[[4](#pone.0174511.ref004)]. However, a large number of patients continue to suffer recurrent ischaemic events[[5](#pone.0174511.ref005)], and this clinical phenomenon has been correlated with lesser degrees of platelet inhibition[[6](#pone.0174511.ref006)]. This failure of the antiplatelet drug to inhibit its target of action is called clopidogrel non-responsiveness or clopidogrel resistance[[7](#pone.0174511.ref007)]. Recently, both prasugrel and ticagrelor, which are novel and stronger antiplatelet agents, were shown to exert more consistent, rapid and effective P2Y12 receptor inhibition in patients with acute coronary syndrome (ACS)[[8](#pone.0174511.ref008)]. Nevertheless, high incidence of major bleeding in some patients receiving prasugrel was noted[[9](#pone.0174511.ref009)], and ticagrelor was associated with an 11% increase in combined major and minor PLATO bleeding rates after careful analysis of bleeding events[[10](#pone.0174511.ref010)]. And cases of inadequate platelet inhibition of prasugrel had been occasionally reported however the incidence of this is less than clopidogrel resistance and this is in keeping with prasugrel being a prodrug[[11](#pone.0174511.ref011)]. Therefore, clopidogrel remains to be one of the most extensively prescribed antiplatelet drugs in CAD patients, and research focused on the individual susceptibility to clopidogrel is of vital significance.\n    22→\n    23→Several clinical and demographic factors may influence the antiplatelet efficacy of clopidogrel, such as drug-drug interactions (such as Proton pump inhibitors[[12](#pone.0174511.ref012)]), renal dysfunction, diabetes mellitus (DM), diet, smoking, age, reduced left ventricular function, inflammation and the presence of an ACS[[13](#pone.0174511.ref013)]. However, genetic factors, specifically the expression of the ABCB1 gene, may significantly influence clopidogrel’s response[[14](#pone.0174511.ref014)].\n    24→\n    25→Clopidogrel is an oral, second-generation thienopyridine irreversible inhibitor of the P2Y12 receptor. It undergoes rapid absorption by the duodenum and is metabolized by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes. About 15% of clopidogrel’s prodrug is converted into a biologically active thiol metabolite, which, in circulation, irreversibly combines to and inactivates the P2Y12 receptor on the surfaces of platelets, resulting in the inhibition of ADP-induced platelet activation and aggregation[[15](#pone.0174511.ref015)]. In the above transformation, specific genetic variants are responsible for clopidogrel’s transport (ATP-binding cassette subfamily B member 1 [ABCB1]), metabolism (CYP enzymes, paraoxonase-1) and action (P2Y12)[[16](#pone.0174511.ref016)].\n    26→\n    27→The *ABCB1* gene, which is also called *MDR1* or *TAP1*, encodes the intestinal efflux transporter pump P-glycoprotein and modulates the absorption of clopidogrel[[17](#pone.0174511.ref017)]. The effect of different levels of ABCB1 expression is unclear. Simon *et al*.[[18](#pone.0174511.ref018)] first analysed the influence of the ABCB1 C3435T (rs1045642) polymorphism on clinical outcomes in persons treating with clopidogrel and found that homozygous patients had a higher risk of cardiovascular events. One recent study was found that exposition to clopidogrel, measured by AUC0-t of the drug, was significantly lower in TT homozygotes comparing to CC and CT genotypes (ABCB1 3435C>T), which showed that the presence of 3435C>T allele had an impact on clopidogrel pharmacokinetics[[19](#pone.0174511.ref019)]. However, these findings could not be confirmed in several subsequent studies. Additionally, the effect of the rs1128503 polymorphism and the DNA methylation of selected CpG islands in the ABCB1 gene on clopidogrel’s response are poorly understood. Thus, in the present study, we attempted to assess whether the rs1045642 and rs1128503 polymorphisms and DNA methylation in the ABCB1 promoter are involved in clopidogrel resistance in Chinese CAD patients treated with clopidogrel.\n    28→\n    29→## Methods\n    30→\n    31→### Study population\n    32→\n    33→From 2010 to 2015, a total of 180 patients with acute coronary syndromes (ACS) were enrolled in present study in the Ningbo No. 1 Hospital. All of them were Han Chinese from Ningbo city in Eastern China. The inclusion criteria were as follows: (1) According to the most recent guidelines (ACC/AHA guidelines), all individuals were undergoing PCI through the radial route via a drug-eluting stent. The patients we tested the platelet function were mostly existed multi-vessel or left main vessel disease of the coronary artery. (2) The patients received clopidogrel (300 mg) and aspirin (300 mg) before PCI and were administered clopidogrel 75 mg and aspirin 100 mg daily. (3) The patients’ age was required to be ≥18 years. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) the therapy of concomitant glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor or warfarin, (2) hepatic or renal insufficiency, (3) history of active bleeding diathesis, (4) hepatic and renal insufficiency, and (5) total platelet count < 150 000 μl^-1^ or > 500 000 μl^-1^. All patients provided written informed consent before participation in the research. The study protocol was reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of Ningbo No1. Hospital and conformed to the guide lines of the Declaration of Helsinki.\n    34→\n    35→### Collection of blood samples and clinical data\n    36→\n    37→Blood samples were obtained overnight via venipuncture in the antecubital area. Serologic markers, such as the concentrations of TC, TG, LDL, GLU, HbA1c, BUN, and CREA, were detected. All the measures applied the standard procedures, and data were collected.\n    38→\n    39→### Platelet function measurements\n    40→\n    41→With the administration of clopidogrel for 3 to 5 days, the platelet reactivity may have been stable or not significantly changed in AMI patients undergoing PCI[[20](#pone.0174511.ref020)]. We detected the platelet reactivity one month after PCI. By the double-syringe technique, blood samples were gathered and the first 5 ml was discarded to avert unprompted platelet activation. The platelet function measurements were analysed by the VerifyNow *P2Y12* assay (Accumetrics Inc., San Diego, California), which was developed to assess the response to *P2Y12* antagonists[[21](#pone.0174511.ref021)]. The VerifyNow *P2Y12* assay reported *P2Y12* reaction units (PRU), and a PRU more than 240 reaction units suggested the existence of clopidogrel resistance[[22](#pone.0174511.ref022)].\n    42→\n    43→### Genomic DNA extraction, genotyping, and methylation assay\n    44→\n    45→Human genomic DNA was extracted from 3 ml peripheral blood by QIAamp DNA BloodMini Kit (Qiagen). Samples were stored at -100°C until use. PCR primers were planned through PyroMark Assay Design software. The sequences of primers used in the SNP genotyping and DNA Methylation Assay are described in **Table 1**.\n    46→\n    47→### Table 1. The primers for SNP genotyping and DNA methylation assay.\n    48→\n    49→|   | Group | DNA sequence |\n    50→| --- | --- | --- |\n    51→| SNP genotyping | rs1045642-F | 5-'GTGTGCTGGTCCTGAAGTTG-3' |\n    52→|   | rs1045642-R | 5'- TGGAGCCTCAAGCCTATAGC -3' |\n    53→|   | rs1128503-F | 5-'GTTCACTTCAGTTACCCATCTCG-3' |\n    54→|   | rs1128503-R | 5'- CGTGGTGGCAAACAATACAGG -3' |\n    55→| DNA Methylation Assay | ABCB1-F1 | GGATATGGAAGTTAAGATTTTAGAGATA |\n    56→|   | ABCB1-R1 | ATTTCAAATATCCCATTACCACATATAAC |\n    57→|   | ABCB1-S1 | ATGATTAATGAGGTAGAAAAAAG |\n    58→Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification (BIO-RAD C1000touch Thermal Cycler PCR) was performed as follows: 50 μl reaction volume containing 1 μl template DNA, 1.5 μl 10 mM dNTP, 5 μl Taq Buffer, 1.0 μl25 mM MgCl_2_, 1.5 μl upstream and downstream primers, 1 μl platinum Taq polymerase 1 U and 37.5 μl water. PCR cycling conditions consisted of 2 cycles of 94°C denaturation for 2 min, 30 cycles of 94°C denaturation for 30 sec, 55°C annealing for 45 sec, and 68°C extension for 3 min, and one cycle of 68°C repair extension for 10 min. PCR products were purified (SK1141; kit Sangon Biotech), measured (3500XL sequence analyser; ABI), and sequenced.\n    59→\n    60→The bisulphite pyrosequencing technology was applied to evaluate the quantitative DNA methylation of 2 CpG dinucleotides on the fragment of ABCB1 gene promoter[[23](#pone.0174511.ref023)]. This process was also combined with sodium bisulphite DNA conversion chemistry (EpiTech Bisulphite Kits; Qiagen), polymerase chain reaction amplification (Pyromark PCR Kit; Qiagen) and sequencing (Pyromark Gold Q24 Reagents; Qiagen) of the target fragment[[24](#pone.0174511.ref024)].\n    61→\n    62→### Statistical analysis\n    63→\n    64→Quantitative data are described as the mean ± standard deviation for categorical variables or the median with interquartile range (IQR) for continuous variables. We carried out a suite of statistical analyses to investigate the association among genetic variables, various confounding factors and clopidogrel resistance. Categorical variables were analysed with either Pearson’s chi-square test or Fisher’s exact test when appropriate. Continuous variance with normality and homogeneity were applied to compare the mean values from *t*-tests. Non-parametric continuous variance was performed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Logistic regression was used to test the interaction of ABCB1 SNP and confounding variables. All the statistical analyses were conducted by PASW Statistics 18.0 software (SPSS, Inc., Somers, NY, USA). A value of P less than 0.05 was considered to indicate a statistically significant difference.\n    65→\n    66→## Results\n    67→\n    68→### Study population\n    69→\n    70→From May 2010 to October 2015, 180 CAD patients who met all requirements were recruited for the present study. UsingVerifyNowP2Y12 assay, 81 patients whose PRU was greater than 240 were defined as having a poor clopidogrel response or clopidogrel resistance. The demographic and clinical characteristics of the cases and controls are summarized in **Table 2**. The clinical information was well matched except for albumin and the ratios of gender and hypertension. Patients with poor clopidogrel response were more likely to be female (cases versus controls: 32.1% versus 15.15%, *P* = 0.007) and have hypertension (cases versus controls: 75.31% versus 58.59%, *P* = 0.018) and lower albumin levels (cases versus controls:38.11 ± 4.38 versus 39.83 ± 5.64, *P* = 0.026). These data indicated that female sex, hypertension and lower albumin levels might increase the risk of clopidogrel resistance.\n    71→\n    72→### Table 2. Comparison between CR and non-CR characteristics.\n    73→\n    74→|   | CR (81) | Non-CR (99) | P value |\n    75→| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n    76→| Hypertension, n(%) | 61 (75.31) | 58 (58.59) | 0.018 |\n    77→| Diabetes mellitus, n(%) | 16 (19.75)) | 22 (22.22) | 0.686 |\n    78→| Hyperlipidemia, n(%) | 32 (39.51) | 44 (44.44) | 0.505 |\n    79→| Current smoking, n(%) | 31 (38.27) | 42 (42.42) | 0.572 |\n    80→| Alcohol abuse, n(%) | 17 (20.99) | 24 (24.24) | 0.604 |\n    81→| Gender (male), n(%) | 55 (67.90) | 84 (84.85) | 0.007 |\n    82→| Age, y | 65.69 ±11.85 | 63.01 ±12.06 | 0.136 |\n    83→| BMI, kg/m2 | 23.29 ±4.58 | 24.41 ±10.15 | 0.363 |\n    84→| TC, mg/dL | 4.36 ±3.72 | 4.77 ±1.48 | 0.273 |\n    85→| TG, mg/dL | 1.67 ±1.16 | 1.70 ±1.33 | 0.880 |\n    86→| HDL, mg/dL | 0.96 ±0.27 | 1.00 ±0.28 | 0.370 |\n    87→| LDL, mg/dL | 2.77 ±0.98 | 2.86 ±1.02 | 0.533 |\n    88→| GLU, mmol/L | 6.09 ±2.10 | 5.88 ±2.42 | 0.535 |\n    89→| HbA1c, % | 6.17 ±1.08 | 6.49 ±3.36 | 0.420 |\n    90→| ALT, μmol/L | 36.67 ±31.47 | 37.36 ±29.52 | 0.879 |\n    91→| AST, μmol/L | 109.75 ±162.84 | 113.29 ±167.46 | 0.887 |\n    92→| TBIL, μmol/L | 15.29 ±8.51 | 14.77 ±8.03 | 0.676 |\n    93→| Album, g/L | 38.11 ±4.38 | 39.83 ±5.64 | 0.026 |\n    94→| BUN, mmol/L | 5.98 ±2.27 | 5.46 ±1.97 | 0.101 |\n    95→| CREA, mmol/L | 75.37 ±24.93 | 72.71 ±22.44 | 0.453 |\n    96→| UA, μmol/L | 350.88 ±129.78 | 322.37 ±80.42 | 0.073 |\n    97→| hsCRP, mg/L | 12.24 ±19.81 | 14.07 ±26.21 | 0.604 |\n    98→| PLT, *109/L | 194.30 ±58.05 | 201.43 ±73.93 | 0.480 |\n    99→| MPV, fL | 8.38 ±1.32 | 8.09 ±0.93 | 0.088 |\n   100→| PCT, % | 0.16 ±0.04 | 0.16 ±0.05 | 0.953 |\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. Platelets functions were measured using the Verify-Now P2Y12 assay. Applying Polymerase Chain Reaction–Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (PCR-RFLP), the single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) was tested. It was shown that female, hypertension, and lower albumin levels increased the risk of CR (P<0.05). The SNP in rs1045642 was related to all-cause mortality (P = 0.024). We did not find any relationship between the methylation levels of the ABCB1 promoter and CR. In conclusions, our study indicated that ABCB1 polymorphisms might be useful in further evaluating the pathogenesis of CR.\n\n## Introduction\n\nAt sites of vascular injury due to atherosclerotic plaque rupture or erosion, platelets mediate not only haemostasis but also pathologic thrombosis[[1](#pone.0174511.ref001)]. About 15% of clopidogrel’s prodrug is converted into a biologically active thiol metabolite, which, in circulation, irreversibly combines to and inactivates the P2Y12 receptor on the surfaces of platelets, resulting in the inhibition of ADP-induced platelet activation and aggregation[[15](#pone.0174511.ref015)]. Simon *et al*.[[18](#pone.0174511.ref018)] first analysed the influence of the ABCB1 C3435T (rs1045642) polymorphism on clinical outcomes in persons treating with clopidogrel and found that homozygous patients had a higher risk of cardiovascular events. One recent study was found that exposition to clopidogrel, measured by AUC0-t of the drug, was significantly lower in TT homozygotes comparing to CC and CT genotypes (ABCB1 3435C>T), which showed that the presence of 3435C>T allele had an impact on clopidogrel pharmacokinetics[[19](#pone.0174511.ref019)]. However, these findings could not be confirmed in several subsequent studies. Additionally, the effect of the rs1128503 polymorphism and the DNA methylation of selected CpG islands in the ABCB1 gene on clopidogrel’s response are poorly understood. Thus, in the present study, we attempted to assess whether the rs1045642 and rs1128503 polymorphisms and DNA methylation in the ABCB1 promoter are involved in clopidogrel resistance in Chinese CAD patients treated with clopidogrel.\n\n## Methods\n\n### Study population\n\nFrom 2010 to 2015, a total of 180 patients with acute coronary syndromes (ACS) were enrolled in present study in the Ningbo No. 1 Hospital. All of them were Han Chinese from Ningbo city in Eastern China. The inclusion criteria were as follows: (1) According to the most recent guidelines (ACC/AHA guidelines), all individuals were undergoing PCI through the radial route via a drug-eluting stent. The patients we tested the platelet function were mostly existed multi-vessel or left main vessel disease of the coronary artery. (2) The patients received clopidogrel (300 mg) and aspirin (300 mg) before PCI and were administered clopidogrel 75 mg and aspirin 100 mg daily. (3) The patients’ age was required to be ≥18 years. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) the therapy of concomitant glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor or warfarin, (2) hepatic or renal insufficiency, (3) history of active bleeding diathesis, (4) hepatic and renal insufficiency, and (5) total platelet count < 150 000 μl^-1^ or > 500 000 μl^-1^. All patients provided written informed consent before participation in the research. The study protocol was reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of Ningbo No1. Hospital and conformed to the guide lines of the Declaration of Helsinki.\n\n### Collection of blood samples and clinical data\n\nBlood samples were obtained overnight via venipuncture in the antecubital area. Serologic markers, such as the concentrations of TC, TG, LDL, GLU, HbA1c, BUN, and CREA, were detected. All the measures applied the standard procedures, and data were collected.\n\n### Platelet function measurements\n\nWith the administration of clopidogrel for 3 to 5 days, the platelet reactivity may have been stable or not significantly changed in AMI patients undergoing PCI[[20](#pone.0174511.ref020)]. We detected the platelet reactivity one month after PCI. By the double-syringe technique, blood samples were gathered and the first 5 ml was discarded to avert unprompted platelet activation. The platelet function measurements were analysed by the VerifyNow *P2Y12* assay (Accumetrics Inc., San Diego, California), which was developed to assess the response to *P2Y12* antagonists[[21](#pone.0174511.ref021)]. The VerifyNow *P2Y12* assay reported *P2Y12* reaction units (PRU), and a PRU more than 240 reaction units suggested the existence of clopidogrel resistance[[22](#pone.0174511.ref022)].\n\n### Genomic DNA extraction, genotyping, and methylation assay\n\nHuman genomic DNA was extracted from 3 ml peripheral blood by QIAamp DNA BloodMini Kit (Qiagen). Samples were stored at -100°C until use. PCR primers were planned through PyroMark Assay Design software. The sequences of primers used in the SNP genotyping and DNA Methylation Assay are described in **Table 1**.\n\n### Table 1. The primers for SNP genotyping and DNA methylation assay.\n\n|   | Group | DNA sequence |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| SNP genotyping | rs1045642-F | 5-'GTGTGCTGGTCCTGAAGTTG-3' |\n|   | rs1045642-R | 5'- TGGAGCCTCAAGCCTATAGC -3' |\n|   | rs1128503-F | 5-'GTTCACTTCAGTTACCCATCTCG-3' |\n|   | rs1128503-R | 5'- CGTGGTGGCAAACAATACAGG -3' |\n| DNA Methylation Assay | ABCB1-F1 | GGATATGGAAGTTAAGATTTTAGAGATA |\n|   | ABCB1-R1 | ATTTCAAATATCCCATTACCACATATAAC |\n|   | ABCB1-S1 | ATGATTAATGAGGTAGAAAAAAG |\nPolymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification (BIO-RAD C1000touch Thermal Cycler PCR) was performed as follows: 50 μl reaction volume containing 1 μl template DNA, 1.5 μl 10 mM dNTP, 5 μl Taq Buffer, 1.0 μl25 mM MgCl_2_, 1.5 μl upstream and downstream primers, 1 μl platinum Taq polymerase 1 U and 37.5 μl water. PCR cycling conditions consisted of 2 cycles of 94°C denaturation for 2 min, 30 cycles of 94°C denaturation for 30 sec, 55°C annealing for 45 sec, and 68°C extension for 3 min, and one cycle of 68°C repair extension for 10 min. PCR products were purified (SK1141; kit Sangon Biotech), measured (3500XL sequence analyser; ABI), and sequenced.\n\nThe bisulphite pyrosequencing technology was applied to evaluate the quantitative DNA methylation of 2 CpG dinucleotides on the fragment of ABCB1 gene promoter[[23](#pone.0174511.ref023)]. This process was also combined with sodium bisulphite DNA conversion chemistry (EpiTech Bisulphite Kits; Qiagen), polymerase chain reaction amplification (Pyromark PCR Kit; Qiagen) and sequencing (Pyromark Gold Q24 Reagents; Qiagen) of the target fragment[[24](#pone.0174511.ref024)].\n\n### Statistical analysis\n\nQuantitative data are described as the mean ± standard deviation for categorical variables or the median with interquartile range (IQR) for continuous variables. We carried out a suite of statistical analyses to investigate the association among genetic variables, various confounding factors and clopidogrel resistance. Categorical variables were analysed with either Pearson’s chi-square test or Fisher’s exact test when appropriate. Continuous variance with normality and homogeneity were applied to compare the mean values from *t*-tests. Non-parametric continuous variance was performed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Logistic regression was used to test the interaction of ABCB1 SNP and confounding variables. All the statistical analyses were conducted by PASW Statistics 18.0 software (SPSS, Inc., Somers, NY, USA). A value of P less than 0.05 was considered to indicate a statistically significant difference.\n\n## Results\n\n### Study population\n\nFrom May 2010 to October 2015, 180 CAD patients who met all requirements were recruited for the present study. UsingVerifyNowP2Y12 assay, 81 patients whose PRU was greater than 240 were defined as having a poor clopidogrel response or clopidogrel resistance. The demographic and clinical characteristics of the cases and controls are summarized in **Table 2**. The clinical information was well matched except for albumin and the ratios of gender and hypertension. Patients with poor clopidogrel response were more likely to be female (cases versus controls: 32.1% versus 15.15%, *P* = 0.007) and have hypertension (cases versus controls: 75.31% versus 58.59%, *P* = 0.018) and lower albumin levels (cases versus controls:38.11 ± 4.38 versus 39.83 ± 5.64, *P* = 0.026). These data indicated that female sex, hypertension and lower albumin levels might increase the risk of clopidogrel resistance.\n\n### Table 2. Comparison between CR and non-CR characteristics.\n\n|   | CR (81) | Non-CR (99) | P value |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Hypertension, n(%) | 61 (75.31) | 58 (58.59) | 0.018 |\n| Diabetes mellitus, n(%) | 16 (19.75)) | 22 (22.22) | 0.686 |\n| Hyperlipidemia, n(%) | 32 (39.51) | 44 (44.44) | 0.505 |\n| Current smoking, n(%) | 31 (38.27) | 42 (42.42) | 0.572 |\n| Alcohol abuse, n(%) | 17 (20.99) | 24 (24.24) | 0.604 |\n| Gender (male), n(%) | 55 (67.90) | 84 (84.85) | 0.007 |\n| Age, y | 65.69 ±11.85 | 63.01 ±12.06 | 0.136 |\n| BMI, kg/m2 | 23.29 ±4.58 | 24.41 ±10.15 | 0.363 |\n| TC, mg/dL | 4.36 ±3.72 | 4.77 ±1.48 | 0.273 |\n| TG, mg/dL | 1.67 ±1.16 | 1.70 ±1.33 | 0.880 |\n| HDL, mg/dL | 0.96 ±0.27 | 1.00 ±0.28 | 0.370 |\n| LDL, mg/dL | 2.77 ±0.98 | 2.86 ±1.02 | 0.533 |\n| GLU, mmol/L | 6.09 ±2.10 | 5.88 ±2.42 | 0.535 |\n| HbA1c, % | 6.17 ±1.08 | 6.49 ±3.36 | 0.420 |\n| ALT, μmol/L | 36.67 ±31.47 | 37.36 ±29.52 | 0.879 |\n| AST, μmol/L | 109.75 ±162.84 | 113.29 ±167.46 | 0.887 |\n| TBIL, μmol/L | 15.29 ±8.51 | 14.77 ±8.03 | 0.676 |\n| Album, g/L | 38.11 ±4.38 | 39.83 ±5.64 | 0.026 |\n| BUN, mmol/L | 5.98 ±2.27 | 5.46 ±1.97 | 0.101 |\n| CREA, mmol/L | 75.37 ±24.93 | 72.71 ±22.44 | 0.453 |\n| UA, μmol/L | 350.88 ±129.78 | 322.37 ±80.42 | 0.073 |\n| hsCRP, mg/L | 12.24 ±19.81 | 14.07 ±26.21 | 0.604 |\n| PLT, *109/L | 194.30 ±58.05 | 201.43 ±73.93 | 0.480 |\n| MPV, fL | 8.38 ±1.32 | 8.09 ±0.93 | 0.088 |\n| PCT, % | 0.16 ±0.04 | 0.16 ±0.05 | 0.953 |","numLines":100,"startLine":1,"totalLines":431}}}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_019dj7BuP3sgypf8e35RmoZN","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Genetic Polymorphisms in Aquaporin 1 as Risk Factors for Malignant Mesothelioma and Biomarkers of Response to Cisplatin Treatment\n     2→\n     3→## Metadata\n     4→**Authors:** Barbara Senk, Katja Goricar, Viljem Kovac, Vita Dolzan, Assoc Prof Alenka Franko\n     5→**Journal:** Radiology and Oncology\n     6→**Date:** 2019 Mar 3\n     7→**DOI:** [10.2478/raon-2019-0009](https://doi.org/10.2478/raon-2019-0009)\n     8→**PMID:** 30840592\n     9→**PMCID:** PMC6411020\n    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6411020/\n    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6411020/pdf/raon-53-096.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6411020/pdf/raon-53-096.pdf)\n    12→\n    13→## Abstract\n    14→\n    15→**Background:** \n    16→Malignant mesothelioma (MM) is an asbestos related aggressive tumor with poor prognosis. The aim of this study was to investigate if aquaporin 1 (AQP1) genetic polymorphisms influence the risk of MM and the response to cisplatin based MM treatment.\n    17→\n    18→**Patients and methods:** \n    19→The case-control study included 231 patients with MM and a control group of 316 healthy blood donors. All subjects were genotyped for three AQP1polymorphisms (rs1049305, rs1476597 and rs28362731). Logistic and Cox regression were used in statistical analysis.\n    20→\n    21→**Results:** \n    22→AQP1 rs1049305 polymorphism was significantly associated with MM risk in dominant model adjusted for gender and age (OR = 0.60, 95% CI = 0.37–0.96, Padj = 0.033). This polymorphism was also significantly associated with cisplatin based treatment related anaemia (unadjusted: OR = 0.49, 95% CI = 0.27–0.90, P = 0.021; adjusted: for CRP: OR = 0.52, 95% CI = 0.27–0.99, P = 0.046), with leukopenia (OR = 2.09, 95% CI = 1.00–4.35, P = 0.049) in dominant model and with thrombocytopenia (OR = 3.06, 95% CI = 1.01–9.28, P = 0.048) and alopecia (OR = 2.92, 95% CI = 1.00–8.46, P = 0.049) in additive model. AQP1 rs28362731 was significantly associated with thrombocytopenia (unadjusted: OR = 3.73, 95% CI = 1.00–13.84, P = 0.049; adjusted for pain: OR = 4.63, 95% CI = 1.13–19.05, P = 0.034) in additive model.\n    23→\n    24→**Conclusions:** \n    25→AQP1 may play a role in the risk of MM. Furthermore, AQP1 genotype information could improve the prediction of MM patients at increased risk for cisplatin toxicity.\n    26→\n    27→Key words: malignant mesothelioma, AQP1, polymorphism, cisplatin\n    28→\n    29→### Background\n    30→\n    31→Malignant mesothelioma (MM) is an asbestos related aggressive tumor with poor prognosis. The aim of this study was to investigate if aquaporin 1 (*AQP1*) genetic polymorphisms influence the risk of MM and the response to cisplatin based MM treatment.\n    32→\n    33→### Patients and methods\n    34→\n    35→The case-control study included 231 patients with MM and a control group of 316 healthy blood donors. All subjects were genotyped for three *AQP1*polymorphisms (rs1049305, rs1476597 and rs28362731). Logistic and Cox regression were used in statistical analysis.\n    36→\n    37→### Results\n    38→\n    39→*AQP1* rs1049305 polymorphism was significantly associated with MM risk in dominant model adjusted for gender and age (OR = 0.60, 95% CI = 0.37–0.96, P_adj_ = 0.033). This polymorphism was also significantly associated with cisplatin based treatment related anaemia (unadjusted: OR = 0.49, 95% CI = 0.27–0.90, P = 0.021; adjusted: for CRP: OR = 0.52, 95% CI = 0.27–0.99, P = 0.046), with leukopenia (OR = 2.09, 95% CI = 1.00–4.35, P = 0.049) in dominant model and with thrombocytopenia (OR = 3.06, 95% CI = 1.01–9.28, P = 0.048) and alopecia (OR = 2.92, 95% CI = 1.00–8.46, P = 0.049) in additive model. *AQP1* rs28362731 was significantly associated with thrombocytopenia (unadjusted: OR = 3.73, 95% CI = 1.00–13.84, P = 0.049; adjusted for pain: OR = 4.63, 95% CI = 1.13–19.05, P = 0.034) in additive model.\n    40→\n    41→### Conclusions\n    42→\n    43→*AQP1* may play a role in the risk of MM. Furthermore, *AQP1* genotype information could improve the prediction of MM patients at increased risk for cisplatin toxicity.\n    44→\n    45→## Introduction\n    46→\n    47→It is generally accepted that the risk of developing diseases and an individual’s response to the treatment may also depend on their genetic characteristics. In this study, we have focused on malignant mesothelioma (MM), which is a very aggressive cancer associated with the exposure to asbestos.[1](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_001)^, ^[2](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_002)^, ^[3](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_003)^, ^[4](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_004) Most frequently it arises from pleura or peritoneum, but can also arise from other serous surfaces.[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005)^, ^[6](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_006)\n    48→\n    49→In Slovenia, the professional exposure to asbestos occurred mainly in asbestos cement industry, in construction, in manufacture of machinery and insulation materials, in maintenance of various means of transport, in textile industry and in other activities.[7](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_007)^, ^[8](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_008)^, ^[9](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_009) Malignant mesothelioma is associated also with exposure to asbestos outside the workplace.[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005)^, ^[8](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_008)^, ^[9](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_009)^, ^[10](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_010) It is estimated that the incidence of MM will remain stable or will even increase in the near future due to the continuous presence of asbestos in buildings and to the long latent period after exposure to asbestos.[11](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_011)^, ^[12](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_012) It is predicted that its incidence in the most industrialized countries will continue to increase until 2020,[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005) or even later.[11](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_011)\n    50→\n    51→Due to an increasing incidence of MM and its poor prognosis, new prognostic and predictive biomarkers are needed.[13](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_013) Symptoms of MM commonly occur only at late stages, therefore novel biomarkers for earlier diagnosis of MM and for establishing the response to treatment might be a promising opportunity for these patients.[14](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_014) Several classes of potential biomarkers of MM have been studied so far, from serum peptides to genetic and epigenetic biomarkers, however with limited success. Among serum biomarkers, soluble peptides related to mesothelin (soluble mesothelin-related peptides, SMRP),[15](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_015) fibulin-3,[16](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_016) survivin[13](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_013) have been studied, however none of them had sufficient predictive value as a standalone biomarker. It has been proposed, that biomarkers from two different molecular classes: protein and miRNA could be used in a combination to improve the biomarker sensitivity and specificity.[14](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_014)\n    52→\n    53→Another approach was to investigate interin-dividual genetic variability in genes coding for key determinants of molecular pathogenesis of MM as potential biomarkers for prediction of the risk of MM as well as treatment response. Several studies have shown that polymorphisms in the genes involved in xenobiotic and oxidative metabolism or in DNA repair processes may play an important role in aetiology and pathogenesis of MM.[17](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_017)^, ^[18](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_018)^, ^[19](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_019) The most commonly studied *GSTM1* null polymorphism showed an increased risk for MM.[17](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_017) Similarly, two variant alleles of *XRCC1* and *XRCC3* were associated with increased risk for MM.[17](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_017) The carriers of at least one polymorphic *NQO1* allele (CT and TT genotypes) had an increased risk of MM compared to those with CC genotype.[19](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_019) A recent study showed also the association of *FTO* variability with MM susceptibility.[20](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_020) On the other hand, *MMP2* polymorphism was suggested to have a protective role in MM.[21](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_021) Furthermore, two of the investigated *MMP9* single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) had significant but opposing effect on time to progression (TTP) and overall survival (OS) in MM.[22](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_022) *FASL-844* polymorphism could predict progression free survival (PFS) in MM patients receiving platinum based chemotherapy.[23](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_023) Polymorphisms in *REV1* and *REV3L* were also associated with the outcome of cisplatin based chemotherapy in MM.[24](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_024) The results of the study showed that DNA repair gene polymorphisms *XRCC1* may modify the response to gemcitabine-platinum combination chemotherapy in MM patients.[18](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_018) Despite such high numbers of genetic factors investigated, the search for potential novel genetic biomarkers continues.\n    54→\n    55→Aquaporins (AQPs) are small transmembrane proteins, which facilitate an osmotically controlled passage of water. Recent research indicated a key role of AQPs in human carcinogenesis.[25](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_025)^, ^[26](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_026)^, ^[27](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_027) All key processes in cancer cells depend on water in the tumour microenvironment, therefore an enhanced transmembrane transmission of water is stimulated in comparison to normal cells. Overexpression of AQPs in the cell lines of the vascular endothelium and tumour cell lines suggests that AQPs may be closely related to the development and progression of a tumour.[28](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_028) In some cancers AQP1 expression was also shown to participate in metastatic processes.[29](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_029) In *AQP1-*knockout mice, xenograft tumour growth and angiogenesis were reduced, and significant necrosis occurred in the tumour tissues.[30](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_030)\n    56→\n    57→The expression of AQP1 in MM tumour cells has been suggested to be an independent prognostic factor favouring survival in MM patients: higher levels of an AQP1 expression only in tumour cells, but not in vascular cells, predicted a better survival.[31](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_031) Higher levels of AQP1 expression were also associated with a better course of the disease in MM, but with worse course of the disease in some other tumours such as breast cancer, melanoma, urothelial and pharyngeal carcinoma.[32](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_032)^, ^[33](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_033)^, ^[34](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_034)^, ^[35](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_035) AQP1 is of interest as a potential biomarker in MM patients as it was shown to be an independent prognostic factor[11](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_011) with high levels of its expression correlating with an increased survival.[29](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_029)^, ^[31](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_031)^, ^[36](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_036) AQP1 expression also correlated with improved survival rates in MM with epithelioid component in comparison to AQP1-poor MM.[37](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_037) Furthermore, AQP1 is also a possible new target for MM treatment,[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005) and there are already AQP1 blockers available which could be used for therapy.[38](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_038)\n    58→\n    59→Genetic polymorphisms were reported in *AQP1* gene, however according to our knowledge they have never been investigated in MM. A functional *AQP1* rs1476597 (-783G/C) SNP leading to transcriptional activation of the *AQP1* promoter and increased *AQP1* mRNA expression in C allele carriers was associated with better survival in glioblastoma multiform patients with GG and GC genotype.[39](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_039) Other *AQP1* SNPs were studied in a variety of conditions, but not in cancer. Firm evidence suggested that *AQP1* rs1049305 SNP could be involved in genetic susceptibility for development of water retention in patients with liver cirrhosis.[40](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_040) The study in marathon runners reported a significant association between *AQP1* rs1049305 and running performance. This study suggested that *AQP1* rs1049305 polymorphism located in 3′-UTR, in interaction with miRNAs could influence the mRNA expression and AQP1 protein levels.[41](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_041) Triathletes who carried *AQP1* rs1049305 C allele had better running performance in comparison to GG genotype. This SNP was not associated with relative body weight change.[41](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_041) It has been suggested that *AQP1* rs10244884 could predict the risk of vaso-occlusion in sickle cell patients.[42](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_042)\n    60→\n    61→The aim of the present study was to investigate the influence of *AQP1* genetic polymorphisms on the risk of developing MM and response to cisplatin-based treatment.\n    62→\n    63→## Patients and methods\n    64→\n    65→### Study population\n    66→\n    67→The case-control study included patients treated for mostly MM of pleura or also peritoneum at the Institute of Oncology Ljubljana from 2007 to the end of 2016. Control group consisted of blood donors from the Institute of Transfusion Medicine in Ljubljana and were over 40 years old.\n    68→\n    69→The diagnosis of MM was made by means of thoracoscopy or video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) in patients with pleural MM and by means of laparoscopy or laparotomy in peritoneal MM. The diagnosis was confirmed histopathologically by an experienced pathologist [15].[15](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_015)\n    70→\n    71→Demographic and clinical data (age, gender, smoking, possible other diseases) from patients with MM were obtained from the medical records of the Institute of Oncology Ljubljana.\n    72→\n    73→The following clinical indicators were used to evaluate the efficacy of treatment: response to treatment according to the modified criteria RECIST (Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours),[43](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_043) PFS and overall survival (OS). The toxicity of the treatment was assessed according to NCI criteria (National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events, version 4.0).[44](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_044)\n    74→\n    75→### Ethical approval\n    76→\n    77→The study was approved by the Republic of Slovenia National Medical Ethics Committee (41/02/09) and was carried out according to the Helsinki Declaration. All the subjects included in the study have signed the written informed consent.\n    78→\n    79→### Genotyping methods\n    80→\n    81→DNA samples from 26 patients were isolated from peripheral venous blood with commercially available reagent sets (QIAamp DNA Mini Kit and Flexigene DNA Kit (Qiagen, Hilden, Germany)). For all other patients and controls DNA was already isolated from peripheral venous blood samples during the course of the previous studies.[18](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_018)^, ^[45](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_045)^, ^[46](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_046)^, ^[47](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_047)^, ^[48](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_048)\n    82→\n    83→Based on the bioinformatics analysis, we selected the following SNPs: *AQP1* rs1049305 G> C in 3’-untranslated region that may affect the binding of miRNA [41], *AQP1* rs1476597 G> C in the 5’-regulatory region that may affect the binding of the transcription factors[49](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_049) and *AQP1* rs28362731 G> A that may affect splicing.\n    84→\n    85→All the polymorphisms were genotyped using competitive allele specific PCR (KASPar) according to the manufacturer’s instructions (LGC Genomics, UK).\n    86→\n    87→### Statistical analysis\n    88→\n    89→All statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics version 21.0 (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). With the usual descriptive statistics we first described the characteristics of each variable separately. In order to assess the causal relationship between MM and the individual variables, we first used a univariate logistic regression. Both additive and dominant models were used to assess the effect of the selected *AQP1* polymorphisms. Analysis was followed by the multivariate statistical modelling, taking into account the selected *AQP1* polymorphisms and possible confounders such as age, gender, and smoking and significant clinical parameters. Hazard ratio (HR), 95% confidence interval (95% CI) and P-value were determined by Cox regression and median survival was determined by the Kaplan-Meier method.\n    90→\n    91→In order to test the interactions between the selected *AQP1* polymorphisms we introduced the logistic regression models with dummy variables.\n    92→\n    93→## Results\n    94→\n    95→The clinical characteristics of MM patients are shown in [Table 1](#j_raon-2019-0009_tab_001). Among all 231 patients whose median (25%–75% range) age was 66 (58–73) years, men represented 73.6%. Epithelioid MM was present in 72.3% of patients. ECOG performance status 1 (48.1%) and 2 (39.0%) prevailed. Exposure to asbestos was confirmed in 73.8% of patients. Among all patients, 46.7% were smokers. In total 194 patients were treated with cisplatin based therapy.\n    96→\n    97→### Table 1.\n    98→\n    99→|   |   | All MM patients | MM patients cisplatin treated based with chemotherapy |\n   100→| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"b5e2a953-9a69-42de-a6ff-64900b33aa2a","uuid":"56b0cd54-2b8c-4ed6-988d-701464cc1d94","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/app/papers/PMC6411020.md","content":"# Genetic Polymorphisms in Aquaporin 1 as Risk Factors for Malignant Mesothelioma and Biomarkers of Response to Cisplatin Treatment\n\n## Metadata\n**Authors:** Barbara Senk, Katja Goricar, Viljem Kovac, Vita Dolzan, Assoc Prof Alenka Franko\n**Journal:** Radiology and Oncology\n**Date:** 2019 Mar 3\n**DOI:** [10.2478/raon-2019-0009](https://doi.org/10.2478/raon-2019-0009)\n**PMID:** 30840592\n**PMCID:** PMC6411020\n**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6411020/\n**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6411020/pdf/raon-53-096.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6411020/pdf/raon-53-096.pdf)\n\n## Abstract\n\n**Background:** \nMalignant mesothelioma (MM) is an asbestos related aggressive tumor with poor prognosis. The aim of this study was to investigate if aquaporin 1 (AQP1) genetic polymorphisms influence the risk of MM and the response to cisplatin based MM treatment.\n\n**Patients and methods:** \nThe case-control study included 231 patients with MM and a control group of 316 healthy blood donors. All subjects were genotyped for three AQP1polymorphisms (rs1049305, rs1476597 and rs28362731). Logistic and Cox regression were used in statistical analysis.\n\n**Results:** \nAQP1 rs1049305 polymorphism was significantly associated with MM risk in dominant model adjusted for gender and age (OR = 0.60, 95% CI = 0.37–0.96, Padj = 0.033). This polymorphism was also significantly associated with cisplatin based treatment related anaemia (unadjusted: OR = 0.49, 95% CI = 0.27–0.90, P = 0.021; adjusted: for CRP: OR = 0.52, 95% CI = 0.27–0.99, P = 0.046), with leukopenia (OR = 2.09, 95% CI = 1.00–4.35, P = 0.049) in dominant model and with thrombocytopenia (OR = 3.06, 95% CI = 1.01–9.28, P = 0.048) and alopecia (OR = 2.92, 95% CI = 1.00–8.46, P = 0.049) in additive model. AQP1 rs28362731 was significantly associated with thrombocytopenia (unadjusted: OR = 3.73, 95% CI = 1.00–13.84, P = 0.049; adjusted for pain: OR = 4.63, 95% CI = 1.13–19.05, P = 0.034) in additive model.\n\n**Conclusions:** \nAQP1 may play a role in the risk of MM. Furthermore, AQP1 genotype information could improve the prediction of MM patients at increased risk for cisplatin toxicity.\n\nKey words: malignant mesothelioma, AQP1, polymorphism, cisplatin\n\n### Background\n\nMalignant mesothelioma (MM) is an asbestos related aggressive tumor with poor prognosis. The aim of this study was to investigate if aquaporin 1 (*AQP1*) genetic polymorphisms influence the risk of MM and the response to cisplatin based MM treatment.\n\n### Patients and methods\n\nThe case-control study included 231 patients with MM and a control group of 316 healthy blood donors. All subjects were genotyped for three *AQP1*polymorphisms (rs1049305, rs1476597 and rs28362731). Logistic and Cox regression were used in statistical analysis.\n\n### Results\n\n*AQP1* rs1049305 polymorphism was significantly associated with MM risk in dominant model adjusted for gender and age (OR = 0.60, 95% CI = 0.37–0.96, P_adj_ = 0.033). This polymorphism was also significantly associated with cisplatin based treatment related anaemia (unadjusted: OR = 0.49, 95% CI = 0.27–0.90, P = 0.021; adjusted: for CRP: OR = 0.52, 95% CI = 0.27–0.99, P = 0.046), with leukopenia (OR = 2.09, 95% CI = 1.00–4.35, P = 0.049) in dominant model and with thrombocytopenia (OR = 3.06, 95% CI = 1.01–9.28, P = 0.048) and alopecia (OR = 2.92, 95% CI = 1.00–8.46, P = 0.049) in additive model. *AQP1* rs28362731 was significantly associated with thrombocytopenia (unadjusted: OR = 3.73, 95% CI = 1.00–13.84, P = 0.049; adjusted for pain: OR = 4.63, 95% CI = 1.13–19.05, P = 0.034) in additive model.\n\n### Conclusions\n\n*AQP1* may play a role in the risk of MM. Furthermore, *AQP1* genotype information could improve the prediction of MM patients at increased risk for cisplatin toxicity.\n\n## Introduction\n\nIt is generally accepted that the risk of developing diseases and an individual’s response to the treatment may also depend on their genetic characteristics. In this study, we have focused on malignant mesothelioma (MM), which is a very aggressive cancer associated with the exposure to asbestos.[1](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_001)^, ^[2](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_002)^, ^[3](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_003)^, ^[4](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_004) Most frequently it arises from pleura or peritoneum, but can also arise from other serous surfaces.[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005)^, ^[6](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_006)\n\nIn Slovenia, the professional exposure to asbestos occurred mainly in asbestos cement industry, in construction, in manufacture of machinery and insulation materials, in maintenance of various means of transport, in textile industry and in other activities.[7](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_007)^, ^[8](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_008)^, ^[9](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_009) Malignant mesothelioma is associated also with exposure to asbestos outside the workplace.[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005)^, ^[8](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_008)^, ^[9](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_009)^, ^[10](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_010) It is estimated that the incidence of MM will remain stable or will even increase in the near future due to the continuous presence of asbestos in buildings and to the long latent period after exposure to asbestos.[11](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_011)^, ^[12](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_012) It is predicted that its incidence in the most industrialized countries will continue to increase until 2020,[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005) or even later.[11](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_011)\n\nDue to an increasing incidence of MM and its poor prognosis, new prognostic and predictive biomarkers are needed.[13](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_013) Symptoms of MM commonly occur only at late stages, therefore novel biomarkers for earlier diagnosis of MM and for establishing the response to treatment might be a promising opportunity for these patients.[14](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_014) Several classes of potential biomarkers of MM have been studied so far, from serum peptides to genetic and epigenetic biomarkers, however with limited success. Among serum biomarkers, soluble peptides related to mesothelin (soluble mesothelin-related peptides, SMRP),[15](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_015) fibulin-3,[16](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_016) survivin[13](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_013) have been studied, however none of them had sufficient predictive value as a standalone biomarker. It has been proposed, that biomarkers from two different molecular classes: protein and miRNA could be used in a combination to improve the biomarker sensitivity and specificity.[14](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_014)\n\nAnother approach was to investigate interin-dividual genetic variability in genes coding for key determinants of molecular pathogenesis of MM as potential biomarkers for prediction of the risk of MM as well as treatment response. Several studies have shown that polymorphisms in the genes involved in xenobiotic and oxidative metabolism or in DNA repair processes may play an important role in aetiology and pathogenesis of MM.[17](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_017)^, ^[18](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_018)^, ^[19](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_019) The most commonly studied *GSTM1* null polymorphism showed an increased risk for MM.[17](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_017) Similarly, two variant alleles of *XRCC1* and *XRCC3* were associated with increased risk for MM.[17](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_017) The carriers of at least one polymorphic *NQO1* allele (CT and TT genotypes) had an increased risk of MM compared to those with CC genotype.[19](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_019) A recent study showed also the association of *FTO* variability with MM susceptibility.[20](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_020) On the other hand, *MMP2* polymorphism was suggested to have a protective role in MM.[21](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_021) Furthermore, two of the investigated *MMP9* single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) had significant but opposing effect on time to progression (TTP) and overall survival (OS) in MM.[22](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_022) *FASL-844* polymorphism could predict progression free survival (PFS) in MM patients receiving platinum based chemotherapy.[23](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_023) Polymorphisms in *REV1* and *REV3L* were also associated with the outcome of cisplatin based chemotherapy in MM.[24](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_024) The results of the study showed that DNA repair gene polymorphisms *XRCC1* may modify the response to gemcitabine-platinum combination chemotherapy in MM patients.[18](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_018) Despite such high numbers of genetic factors investigated, the search for potential novel genetic biomarkers continues.\n\nAquaporins (AQPs) are small transmembrane proteins, which facilitate an osmotically controlled passage of water. Recent research indicated a key role of AQPs in human carcinogenesis.[25](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_025)^, ^[26](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_026)^, ^[27](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_027) All key processes in cancer cells depend on water in the tumour microenvironment, therefore an enhanced transmembrane transmission of water is stimulated in comparison to normal cells. Overexpression of AQPs in the cell lines of the vascular endothelium and tumour cell lines suggests that AQPs may be closely related to the development and progression of a tumour.[28](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_028) In some cancers AQP1 expression was also shown to participate in metastatic processes.[29](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_029) In *AQP1-*knockout mice, xenograft tumour growth and angiogenesis were reduced, and significant necrosis occurred in the tumour tissues.[30](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_030)\n\nThe expression of AQP1 in MM tumour cells has been suggested to be an independent prognostic factor favouring survival in MM patients: higher levels of an AQP1 expression only in tumour cells, but not in vascular cells, predicted a better survival.[31](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_031) Higher levels of AQP1 expression were also associated with a better course of the disease in MM, but with worse course of the disease in some other tumours such as breast cancer, melanoma, urothelial and pharyngeal carcinoma.[32](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_032)^, ^[33](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_033)^, ^[34](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_034)^, ^[35](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_035) AQP1 is of interest as a potential biomarker in MM patients as it was shown to be an independent prognostic factor[11](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_011) with high levels of its expression correlating with an increased survival.[29](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_029)^, ^[31](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_031)^, ^[36](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_036) AQP1 expression also correlated with improved survival rates in MM with epithelioid component in comparison to AQP1-poor MM.[37](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_037) Furthermore, AQP1 is also a possible new target for MM treatment,[5](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_005) and there are already AQP1 blockers available which could be used for therapy.[38](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_038)\n\nGenetic polymorphisms were reported in *AQP1* gene, however according to our knowledge they have never been investigated in MM. A functional *AQP1* rs1476597 (-783G/C) SNP leading to transcriptional activation of the *AQP1* promoter and increased *AQP1* mRNA expression in C allele carriers was associated with better survival in glioblastoma multiform patients with GG and GC genotype.[39](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_039) Other *AQP1* SNPs were studied in a variety of conditions, but not in cancer. Firm evidence suggested that *AQP1* rs1049305 SNP could be involved in genetic susceptibility for development of water retention in patients with liver cirrhosis.[40](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_040) The study in marathon runners reported a significant association between *AQP1* rs1049305 and running performance. This study suggested that *AQP1* rs1049305 polymorphism located in 3′-UTR, in interaction with miRNAs could influence the mRNA expression and AQP1 protein levels.[41](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_041) Triathletes who carried *AQP1* rs1049305 C allele had better running performance in comparison to GG genotype. This SNP was not associated with relative body weight change.[41](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_041) It has been suggested that *AQP1* rs10244884 could predict the risk of vaso-occlusion in sickle cell patients.[42](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_042)\n\nThe aim of the present study was to investigate the influence of *AQP1* genetic polymorphisms on the risk of developing MM and response to cisplatin-based treatment.\n\n## Patients and methods\n\n### Study population\n\nThe case-control study included patients treated for mostly MM of pleura or also peritoneum at the Institute of Oncology Ljubljana from 2007 to the end of 2016. Control group consisted of blood donors from the Institute of Transfusion Medicine in Ljubljana and were over 40 years old.\n\nThe diagnosis of MM was made by means of thoracoscopy or video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) in patients with pleural MM and by means of laparoscopy or laparotomy in peritoneal MM. The diagnosis was confirmed histopathologically by an experienced pathologist [15].[15](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_015)\n\nDemographic and clinical data (age, gender, smoking, possible other diseases) from patients with MM were obtained from the medical records of the Institute of Oncology Ljubljana.\n\nThe following clinical indicators were used to evaluate the efficacy of treatment: response to treatment according to the modified criteria RECIST (Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours),[43](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_043) PFS and overall survival (OS). The toxicity of the treatment was assessed according to NCI criteria (National Cancer Institute Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events, version 4.0).[44](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_044)\n\n### Ethical approval\n\nThe study was approved by the Republic of Slovenia National Medical Ethics Committee (41/02/09) and was carried out according to the Helsinki Declaration. All the subjects included in the study have signed the written informed consent.\n\n### Genotyping methods\n\nDNA samples from 26 patients were isolated from peripheral venous blood with commercially available reagent sets (QIAamp DNA Mini Kit and Flexigene DNA Kit (Qiagen, Hilden, Germany)). For all other patients and controls DNA was already isolated from peripheral venous blood samples during the course of the previous studies.[18](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_018)^, ^[45](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_045)^, ^[46](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_046)^, ^[47](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_047)^, ^[48](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_048)\n\nBased on the bioinformatics analysis, we selected the following SNPs: *AQP1* rs1049305 G> C in 3’-untranslated region that may affect the binding of miRNA [41], *AQP1* rs1476597 G> C in the 5’-regulatory region that may affect the binding of the transcription factors[49](#j_raon-2019-0009_ref_049) and *AQP1* rs28362731 G> A that may affect splicing.\n\nAll the polymorphisms were genotyped using competitive allele specific PCR (KASPar) according to the manufacturer’s instructions (LGC Genomics, UK).\n\n### Statistical analysis\n\nAll statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics version 21.0 (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). With the usual descriptive statistics we first described the characteristics of each variable separately. In order to assess the causal relationship between MM and the individual variables, we first used a univariate logistic regression. Both additive and dominant models were used to assess the effect of the selected *AQP1* polymorphisms. Analysis was followed by the multivariate statistical modelling, taking into account the selected *AQP1* polymorphisms and possible confounders such as age, gender, and smoking and significant clinical parameters. Hazard ratio (HR), 95% confidence interval (95% CI) and P-value were determined by Cox regression and median survival was determined by the Kaplan-Meier method.\n\nIn order to test the interactions between the selected *AQP1* polymorphisms we introduced the logistic regression models with dummy variables.\n\n## Results\n\nThe clinical characteristics of MM patients are shown in [Table 1](#j_raon-2019-0009_tab_001). Among all 231 patients whose median (25%–75% range) age was 66 (58–73) years, men represented 73.6%. Epithelioid MM was present in 72.3% of patients. ECOG performance status 1 (48.1%) and 2 (39.0%) prevailed. Exposure to asbestos was confirmed in 73.8% of patients. Among all patients, 46.7% were smokers. In total 194 patients were treated with cisplatin based therapy.\n\n### Table 1.\n\n|   |   | All MM patients | MM patients cisplatin treated based with chemotherapy |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |","numLines":100,"startLine":1,"totalLines":389}}}
